2026-06-09T08:47:33.7038165Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7038586Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7039115Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7039621Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7040165Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7040594Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7041362Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7042015Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7042615Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7043062Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7043436Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7043860Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7044240Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7044742Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7045294Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7045793Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7046251Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7046734Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7047133Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7047375Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7047728Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7048163Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7048727Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7049234Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:33.7049733Z [36;1m  fi[0m

2026-06-09T08:47:50.0275085Z [36;1m# Generate a secure random API key (360 bits of entropy, 40+ chars)[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:50.0275572Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:50.0276004Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:50.0276343Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:50.0276606Z [36;1m[0m

2026-06-09T08:47:57.9419605Z   GH_AW_MODEL_MULTIPLIERS: {
  "version": "1",
  "description": "Effective Tokens (ET) computation data per the gh-aw Effective Tokens Specification v0.2.0. The registry keeps complete model history; entries are removed only by explicit manual deletion.",

2026-06-09T08:52:48.2710538Z The analysis is complete. The verbose-mode `rendered_json` logging (services.py:140-141) dumps the full post-rewrite hook JSON to the terminal — content that originates entirely from package-provided hook files, not APM's credential chain. APM injects no tokens into hook commands, and whatever appears in that JSON was already written to disk by the pre-existing hook integration. No APM auth surface is touched.

2026-06-09T08:57:28.1640096Z       "rationale": "json.load returns actual ESC bytes (0x1b) from \\u001b-encoded hook JSON. _summarize_command returns the raw Python string (with ESC bytes) via 'runs {command}'. _log_hook_display_payloads prints event name and summary directly to terminal without stripping control characters. By contrast, _emit_hook_event_diagnostics passes the same event-name data through _sanitize_event_name (0x20-0x7E filter) before logging. A crafted package hook with ANSI escape sequences in its event name or bash/command value can manipulate terminal output during install, clearing the screen or printing misleading status lines that suppress visibility of what is being deployed. The verbose rendered_json path is safe because json.dumps re-escapes 0x1b to \\u001b before printing.",

2026-06-09T09:04:22.5241247Z {
2026-06-09T09:04:22.5893135Z   "headline": "Hook transparency delivers APM's strongest enterprise trust signal, but Claude/Gemini rendered_json divergence and a 3-of-6 HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS blindspot break the PR's stated security invariant before it can ship.",
2026-06-09T09:04:32.9908790Z   "arbitration": "This PR delivers what APM has been missing as an enterprise differentiator: a user-visible, per-event audit trail of exactly what each hook does at the moment it deploys. The oss-growth-hacker is correct that this is APM's strongest enterprise trust signal since the content scanner, and the core architecture (building `display_payloads` from post-rewrite data, the integration-tier test structure) is well-conceived. harshitlarl's community contribution rebased with authorship preserved is exemplary OSS hygiene that should be celebrated explicitly in the release note.\n\nThree concrete bugs make the stated security invariant false before merging. First, for Claude targets, `_build_display_payload` is called after `entry[_apm_source] = source_marker` mutates `rewritten` in-place, but before that key is stripped for the disk write in lines 1489–1494 -- so `rendered_json` carries `_apm_source` keys absent from the deployed `.claude/settings.json`. Second, for Gemini targets, `display_payloads` is built from pre-`_to_gemini_hook_entries`-transform data while the disk holds the renamed (`bash`→`command`), rewrapped, and millisecond-converted Gemini format -- a different schema than what the user sees. Both are direct violations of the PR's own invariant: 'display_payloads faithfully reflects what is written to disk and executed.' Third, four panelists independently identify that `_iter_hook_entries` checks only 3 of the 6 `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS` -- hooks using `windows`, `linux`, or `osx` keys deploy correctly but produce zero transparency output, the precise failure mode the PR was built to prevent. The test-coverage-expert's `outcome: missing` on `_log_hook_display_payloads` (the headline user-visible function) compounds all three bugs: there is no automated guardrail that would catch a regression in the very output this PR ships as a security promise.\n\nThe remediation is bounded and concrete -- no design change required. (1) Snapshot `rewritten` before the `_apm_source` annotation loop, or pass `copy.deepcopy(rewritten)` to `_build_display_payload`, to fix the Claude invariant. (2) Build Gemini display payloads from post-`_to_gemini_hook_entries` data. (3) Expand `_iter_hook_entries` and `_summarize_command` to iterate over all of `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS`. (4) Apply the existing `_sanitize_event_name` filter to `event_name` and `command` strings in `_build_display_payload` to close the ANSI injection surface flagged by supply-chain-security. (5) Add a minimal test for `_log_hook_display_payloads` with a mock `log_fn`. This is a targeted patch pass, not a redesign.",

2026-06-09T09:04:59.9786574Z     "stance": "needs_rework",
2026-06-09T09:05:02.7791370Z     "prose": "The Claude and Gemini `rendered_json` divergence bugs and the 3-of-6 `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS` blindspot collectively make the PR's stated security invariant false on this commit -- shipping it as a trust signal before fixing these would be worse than not shipping it. The required changes are a bounded patch pass: snapshot `rewritten` before `_apm_source` mutation, build Gemini payloads post-transform, expand the key loop to all six `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS`, apply the existing sanitizer to display output, and add a minimal rendering test. Once those five items land, this PR becomes APM's most compelling enterprise-trust story and should ship fast with the CHANGELOG and docs updates the doc-writer and oss-growth-hacker identified."

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5522120Z [194] ◐ Now let me look more specifically at the `_log_hook_display_payloads` function and the `display_payloads` rendering in `services.py` to see if there's any chance of credential/token exposure.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5522899Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5523288Z   The key concern would be: can hook commands in `display_payloads` contain tokens that get logged?
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5523742Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5524019Z   From the test file, I can see the hook commands are things like:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5524439Z   - `python3 ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/pretooluse.py`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5524763Z   - `echo hello`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5525153Z   - `bash /scripts/run.sh`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5525392Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5525734Z   These are command strings from the hook JSON files (authored by package publishers). 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5526152Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5528712Z   If a hook command string contained an environment variable like `$GITHUB_TOKEN` or a literal token, it would get logged to the console. That said, this is more of a supply-chain risk where malicious hook authors could intentionally log environment variables through their commands, rather than an issue with how APM itself constructs or handles these commands. APM doesn't generate the hook commands—they come directly from the package-provided hook JSON files. Let me examine the `_log_hook_display_payloads` function and the `rendered_json` field more closely to understand what's actually being displayed.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5531388Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5533102Z   The function logs per-hook-file action summaries for transparency, using the post-path-rewrite data from `display_payloads`. The structure includes the source hook file path, the output path where the hook JSON gets written, and a list of actions with their event and summary information. In verbose mode, the full hook JSON is logged through the `rendered_json` field, which means any hardcoded tokens in the command strings from package publishers would end up in the logs.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5535044Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5536815Z   But this isn't an APM authentication issue—it's a supply-chain concern since APM doesn't inject tokens into these commands. The hook display path is purely filesystem operations reading and reformatting JSON files written by packages, and APM's own token resolution through AuthResolver isn't involved here. Looking at the `_summarize_command` function, it just extracts fil... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2541 chars]

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5572451Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5572759Z [221] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/in..., view_range: [1, 80])
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5573094Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5573343Z [222] ◐ Now I have a clear picture of the entire auth surface in this PR:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5573709Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5573906Z   1. **`hook_integrator.py`**: 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5574408Z      - `_iter_hook_entries`: Iterates through hook event entries in a JSON payload dict - no auth involvement
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5575401Z      - `_summarize_command`: Extracts a "path token" (filesystem path component) from a command string - this uses "token" in the sense of a word/token from a string, NOT an authentication token
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5576818Z      - `_build_display_payload`: Builds a dict with `source_hook_file`, `output_path`, `actions` (list of event/summary pairs), and `rendered_json` (the full JSON). The `rendered_json` comes from `rewritten` - the post-path-rewrite hook data. 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5577738Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5577926Z   2. **`services.py`**:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5579630Z      - `_log_hook_display_payloads`: In verbose mode, logs `rendered_json` line by line to `verbose_detail`. The content comes entirely from the package's hook JSON files (not APM auth infrastructure). If a package author embedded a literal token in their hook command, it would appear in verbose output - but this is the same content that gets written to disk anyway (the pre-existing hook integration behavior). This PR just adds transparency logging of what's already being deployed.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5581728Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5582033Z   3. **`base_` (truncated)**: Still examining the remaining auth surface...
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5582405Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5583961Z   The core flow is straightforward: hook JSON files get path-substituted, converted to display payloads, and logged to the CLI. None of the actual auth mechanisms—token resolution, credential managers, environment variable lookups—are involved in this chain. The transparency logging just exposes what's already being written to disk.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5585256Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5586459Z   Even if a package hook contained hardcoded credentials (which would be a supply-chain issue, not an APM auth problem), those were already persisted before this PR. APM itself doesn't inject credentials into hooks, and verbose output of hook commands is orthogonal to the authentication architecture. This is clearly `active: false`.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5587644Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5589262Z [223] ◆ The analysis is complete. The verbose-mode `rendered_json` logging (services.py:140-141) dumps the full post-rewrite hook JSON to the terminal — content that originates entirely from package-provided hook files, not APM's credential chain. APM injects no tokens into hook commands, and whatever appears in that JSON was already written to disk by the pre-existing hook integration. No APM auth surface is touched.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5591029Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5591219Z   ```json
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5591408Z   {
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5591608Z     "persona": "auth-expert",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5591870Z     "active": false,
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5593312Z     "inactive_reason": "All four changed files (install/services.py, integration/base_integrator.py, integration/hook_integrator.py, tests/unit/test_install_hook_transparency.py) are confined to hook-file path rewriting and CLI display logic; none touch AuthResolver, GitHubTokenManager, token env-var lookup, credential helpers, host classification, or HTTP/git authorization headers.",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5595076Z     "summary": "PR is auth-neutral: hook transparency display does not involve APM credential resolution or token management.",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5595639Z     "findings": []
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5595840Z   }
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5596018Z   ```

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5618661Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5619835Z [236] ◐ Now I have a good understanding of the code. Let me look at how the `_verbose` is computed in the outer context vs. how the `_hook_verbose` is computed in the PR changes, and also look at how `tree_item` renders vs. `verbose_detail`. Let me also check on any pattern for JSON-dump truncation or limits in verbose mode.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5621189Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5621390Z   Key observations so far:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5621630Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5622394Z   1. **Indent/pipe character prefix mismatch**: The existing `_log_integration` lines use `  |--` prefix (e.g., `"  |-- {_verb_phrase} -> {_suffix}"`). The new hook action lines use `  |   ` prefix (3 spaces after the pipe). 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5623218Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5625014Z   2. **Verbose mode check redundancy**: The visual hierarchy makes hook actions appear as sub-items under the `|--` line, which seems intentional. But there's also a potential issue where `_verbose` is derived from `ctx.verbose` on line 283, while the new code tries to get verbose from `logger.verbose` separately—if `ctx` is None but `logger` exists, `_verbose` stays False while `_hook_verbose` could be True, creating an inconsistency.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5626582Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5627637Z   3. **Raw JSON in verbose mode with no truncation**: The verbose dump of full hook JSON has no line count or size limit, so a hooks.json file could balloon to hundreds or thousands of lines. For machine-readable or AI-agent use this is fine per the verbose design principle, but there's no safeguard here.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5628753Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5630669Z   4. **`log_fn` callable pattern vs. established logger pattern**: The function takes `log_fn` as a parameter rather than using the logger directly, which makes sense since it encapsulates the `logger.tree_item()` call. The verbose JSON dump uses `logger.verbose_detail()` (which gates internally on `logger.verbose`), while action summaries use `tree_item` (which isn't verbose-gated). But the verbose check is being done manually by passing `verbose` into the function rather than relying on the logger's internal gating.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5632654Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5633416Z   Actually, `logger.verbose_detail` already checks `if self.verbose:` internally, so passing `verbose` separately and then checking `if verbose and logger is not N... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4045 chars]

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5637210Z [240] ◐ Now I have a much clearer picture. Let me look at the actual output tree with the new hooks summary - I need to understand the structure more.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5637820Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5638037Z   Looking at the diff, the structure will be:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5638329Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5638682Z     |-- 2 hook(s) integrated -> .github/hooks/
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5639069Z     |   PreToolUse: runs .github/hooks/pretooluse.py (hooks.json)
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5639514Z     |   PostToolUse: runs .github/hooks/posttooluse.py (hooks.json)
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5639862Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5640038Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5640273Z   vs the existing structure for other primitives:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5640570Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5640942Z     |-- 2 prompt(s) integrated -> .github/prompts/
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5641296Z     |-- 1 hook(s) integrated -> .claude/settings.json
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5641601Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5641786Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5643092Z   The existing expansion lines use `  |     -> <path>` format. The new hook transparency lines use `  |   ` (3 spaces after `|`) as opposed to the existing `  |--` and `  |     ->` formats. I need to assess the tree visual consistency - the existing tree uses `  |-- ` for top-level primitive rows and `  |     -> ` for multi-target expansion lines, but the new hook lines use a different indentation pattern.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5644464Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5645934Z   The output format shows hook events like `PreToolUse: runs .github/hooks/pretooluse.py (hooks.json)` where the CamelCase event name is followed by the summarized command and the source filename in parentheses. The parenthetical reference to `(hooks.json)` could be misleading since it indicates the source file rather than the destination path, which might confuse users about which hook file is actually deployed.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5647473Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5650271Z   The `_summarize_command` heuristic searches for the first token containing "/" or starting with "." to extract the meaningful part of the command. This works for straightforward cases like `python3 .github/hooks/pretooluse.py` → `runs .github/hooks/pretooluse.py`, but runs into issues with more complex commands like `uv run scripts/validate.py` or shell expansions like `python3 $(realpath ./hooks/run.sh)` where the logic struggles to identify the correct path token. In verbose mode, the hook output prints the full raw JSON, but there's a subtle issue with how verbosity is determined—it checks both the `--verbose` CLI flag and a `verbose`... 2026-06-09T09:07:53.5663116Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5663323Z   **What this PR does:**
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5663666Z   - During `apm install`, when hooks are integrated, the user now sees:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5664040Z     ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5664256Z       |-- hook(s) deployed -> .github/hooks/
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5664643Z       |   PreToolUse: runs .github/hooks/pretooluse.py (hooks.json)
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5665082Z       |   PostToolUse: runs .github/hooks/posttooluse.py (hooks.json)
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5665435Z     ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5665688Z   - In verbose mode, full rewritten hook JSON is printed.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5666131Z   - Tests assert `display_payloads == on-disk content` (security invariant).
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5667842Z   - From a growth perspective, this feature is compelling because it directly demonstrates the "Secure by default" and "Governed by policy" promises in a visible, tangible way—hook transparency becomes a real differentiator for security-conscious users. The clean, readable output format makes the security posture immediately apparent during installation.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5669188Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5670186Z   - The feature also closes a meaningful trust gap by making hook deployment visible at the moment it happens, which is particularly valuable for enterprise buyers. The verbose JSON mode doubles as a powerful auditing tool that could be highlighted in security documentation.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5671359Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5675139Z   - There are some concerns around output formatting that need consideration. The security invariant being tested here—that displayed payloads match what's actually on disk—is genuinely novel in the AI agent ecosystem and worth highlighting in release notes or a blog post. But the documentation is silent on this transparency feature; security-conscious users are asking exactly what APM does about hook visibility, and the answer is now "shows you everything," yet install-packages.md and security-and-supply-chain.md don't mention it. Looking at the output format again, there's room to make the hook deployment tree clearer and more discoverable. I should add a concrete tip for users to review hook contents after installation, and update the security and install documentation to highlight this transparency feature since someone searching for "how do I kn... 2026-06-09T09:07:53.5693600Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5694223Z [254] ◐ Now I need to understand the key security question: Does `_build_display_payload` take `rewritten` (post-path-rewrite) data rather than the original `data`?
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5694896Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5695087Z   From my reading:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5696077Z   1. In `integrate_package_hooks` (Copilot target, line 1109-1151): `_rewrite_hooks_data` is called first (line 1115-1122), producing `rewritten`. Then `_build_display_payload` is called with `rewritten` (line 1144-1151). ✅ Correct.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5696955Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5697268Z   2. In `_integrate_merged_hooks` (Claude/Cursor/Codex targets, line 1292-1431): 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5697791Z      - `_rewrite_hooks_data` is called (line 1292-1300), producing `rewritten`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5698291Z      - Then, `rewritten.get("hooks", {})` is used in the merge loop (line 1303)
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5699195Z      - BUT: **after** the hooks are merged into `json_config["hooks"]`, entries are modified in-place: `_apm_source` markers are added (line 1326-1328), entries may be transformed (Gemini format), etc.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5703528Z      - `_build_display_payload` is called (line 1424-1431) with `rewritten` - BUT at this point, the `rewritten` dict's hooks have been mutated by the merge process, so the display payload reflects those in-place modifications. Gemini converts the entries to a different format, but that's just reassigning the local variable, so it doesn't affect `rewritten`. Then those converted entries get appended to `json_config`'s hooks. The real issue is that at step 4, we're mutating the entry dicts in-place by adding `_apm_source`, and since these dicts are referenced in `rewritten`, those mutations persist in the copy—which means `rewritten` now contains markers that won't actually exist on disk for schema-strict targets. from this package's entries, not the full merged result that actually ends up on disk. So there's a mismatch between what the display shows and what gets persisted.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5706586Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5707946Z   Now I'm checking whether the verbose output could expose sensitive data. The `rendered_json` contains the rewritten hook configuration with absolute paths and environment variable references, but these are just the existing hook content—no tokens or credentials embedded ... 2026-06-09T09:07:53.5711401Z [258] ◐ This is a key finding! The `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS` tuple used in the actual hook integration is:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5711865Z   ```python
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5712084Z   HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS: tuple[str, ...] = (
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5712365Z       "command",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5712568Z       "bash",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5712773Z       "powershell",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5712986Z       "windows",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5713194Z       "linux",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5713386Z       "osx",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5713586Z   )
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5713766Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5713948Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5715471Z   But `_iter_hook_entries` only checks `("command", "bash", "powershell")` - missing `windows`, `linux`, and `osx` keys. This means VS Code OS-specific overrides (`windows`, `linux`, `osx`) will be **silently invisible** in the hook transparency output, even though they ARE being deployed. A hook that uses `"windows": "powershell script.ps1"` and `"linux": "bash script.sh"` would show as `Hook file integrated: hooks.json` with no action details at all.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5717840Z   The same gap exists in `_summarize_command`, which also only checks those three keys. This is a real correctness issue because the transparency feature is meant to reveal what will execute, yet OS-specific commands could run silently without being reported to the user.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5718803Z

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5730423Z [272] ◐ Now I have a very clear picture of the PR's changes and what docs need updating. Let me summarize:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5731033Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5731227Z   **What the PR does:**
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5731875Z   - Adds hook transparency during `apm install`: emits per-event hook action summaries for integrated hooks.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5732529Z   - New output format: `  |   PreToolUse: runs .github/hooks/pretooluse.py (hooks.json)`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5733139Z   - In verbose mode (`--verbose`), also prints the full rendered hook JSON (post-path-rewrite).
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5733763Z   - Refactors the hook payload logging into a `_log_hook_display_payloads` helper function.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5734204Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5734403Z   **What was NOT changed in docs:**
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5735630Z   No documentation was updated in this PR, but there are gaps that need addressing. The `--verbose` flag description in the install command docs is now outdated since it doesn't mention that verbose mode also controls whether the full hook JSON gets printed during installation. That's the main documentation update needed.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5736835Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5741085Z   Additionally, the "Behavior" section of that same install page doesn't cover the new per-hook action summaries that appear during integration, and the hooks-and-commands documentation doesn't explain what producers will see at install time regarding hook transparency output and how `--verbose` affects that visibility. The `--verbose` flag description in install.md needs updating since it now also outputs the fully rendered hook JSON after path rewrites, not just the diagnostic summary. Additionally, the hook action summaries appear inline rather than grouped at the end like other diagnostics, which is a behavioral distinction worth noting in the documentation. The `--verbose` flag also reveals the full rendered JSON post-path-rewrite, which matters for security since it confirms exactly what gets written to disk. Finding 4 is a minor wording issue in the skills guide where the `--verbose` description needs updating. 2026-06-09T09:07:53.5758230Z   **Summary of findings:**
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5758464Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5759771Z   1. **recommended** - `docs/src/content/docs/reference/cli/install.md`: `--verbose` description is incomplete. Currently says "Show per-file paths and full error context in the diagnostic summary." After PR #1700, `--verbose` also causes the full rendered (post-path-rewrite) hook JSON to be printed inline during hook integration. The description is stale/incomplete.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5761258Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5763202Z   2. **recommended** - `docs/src/content/docs/reference/cli/install.md`: The "Behavior" section's "Diagnostic summary" bullet mentions grouping output at the end and using `--verbose` to expand individual file paths - but the new hook action summaries (`  |   PreToolUse: runs .github/hooks/pretooluse.py (hooks.json)`) are printed *inline* during integration, not in the grouped diagnostic summary. This behavior difference isn't documented anywhere. Users installing a package with hooks will see new output lines that aren't explained anywhere in the docs
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5765192Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5767127Z   3. **recommended** - `docs/src/content/docs/producer/author-primitives/hooks-and-commands.md`: The page ends by recommending `apm install --dry-run` to preview. After this PR, running a normal `apm install` also emits per-event hook action summaries showing which events trigger and which scripts run (using post-rewrite paths, so it shows the actual deployed paths). This is specifically valuable for hook authors validating their packages. The page should mention this install-time feedback, and note that `--verbose` shows the full rendered JSON.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5769119Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5769339Z   4. **nit** - `packages/apm-guide/.apm/skills
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5769629Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5770450Z   /apm-usage/commands.md`: The `--verbose` flag description in the command reference table should be updated to reflect that it also outputs the full rendered hook JSON, keeping it consistent with the other documentation updates.

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5786863Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5787209Z [285] ◐ Now I have a solid understanding of the PR. Let me analyze the key architectural aspects:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5787651Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5787934Z   1. **`display_payloads: list` (untyped) in `IntegrationResult`**:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5788526Z      - The field is declared as `display_payloads: list = field(default_factory=list)` without a type parameter.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5789213Z      - Other fields on `IntegrationResult` use precise types: `target_paths: list[Path]`, etc.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5790243Z      - The payload structure is well-documented in the docstring but untyped — callers in `_log_hook_display_payloads` do `.get("source_hook_file", ...)` which assumes dict elements.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5792128Z      - The PR comment documents the expected keys (`target_label`, `output_path`, `source_hook_file`, `actions`, `rendered_json`), but there's no `TypedDict` or dataclass enforcing this contract. This is the core issue: `list` without a type parameter breaks the dataclass's type safety, and it should be at least `list[dict]` or ideally `list[HookDisplayPayload]`.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5793441Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5795340Z   2. **Placement of `_iter_hook_entries`/`_summarize_command`/`_build_display_payload` as static methods**: `_iter_hook_entries` and `_summarize_command` are static and don't use `self`, so they could live as module-level functions, but keeping them in the class makes sense since they're part of the hook domain. `_build_display_payload` is an instance method that only calls these static methods, so it could also be static. Since these are only used by `HookIntegrator` right now, leaving them as class methods is reasonable.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5797175Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5798827Z   The `_log_hook_display_payloads` function in `services.py` is correctly positioned as a module-level private function—it handles rendering logic and shouldn't live in `HookIntegrator`. The type hints for `log_fn` and `logger` should be more specific than `Any`, and the dual-path rendering (using `log_fn` for tree items versus `logger.verbose_detail`) is a bit awkward but follows the existing pattern.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5800293Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5801165Z   For the accumulation safety across targets, `_agg_hook_payloads` is reset at the start of each primitive kind loop iteration, so it correctly accumulates across... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 10762 chars]

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5807838Z   - `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS` has 6 keys: `("command", "bash", "powershell", "windows", "linux", "osx")`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5808379Z   - `_rewrite_hooks_data` uses ALL 6 keys via `self.HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5808923Z   - `_iter_hook_entries` (new) only checks 3: `("command", "bash", "powershell")` - a literal subset
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5809542Z   - `_summarize_command` (new) only checks 3: `("command", "bash", "powershell")` - a literal subset
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5811236Z   - This creates a concrete gap: hooks with `windows`, `linux`, or `osx` keys get their paths rewritten correctly but produce no action in the display summary, so the user only sees a generic integration message instead of the actual command being executed. This is a recommended finding since the transparency feature doesn't fully cover platform-specific hook keys.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5812592Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5813856Z   For the adopted-only condition, the code tracks `_agg_files` as the sum across targets, while `_agg_adopted` is tracked separately and shown in the verb phrase. So when adopted-only re-installs display, they show the hook count and adoption status but not the action detail—which is the correct behavior.

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5818018Z [296] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me analyze the findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5818381Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5818572Z   ## Key Findings
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5818778Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5819212Z   ### 1. `_iter_hook_entries` and `_summarize_command` silently skip OS-specific keys (`windows`, `linux`, `osx`)
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5819703Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5820026Z   The `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS` tuple used for actual rewriting (lines 384-391) includes:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5820433Z   ```python
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5820820Z   "command", "bash", "powershell", "windows", "linux", "osx"
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5821152Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5821337Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5821695Z   But `_iter_hook_entries` (lines 394-420) and `_summarize_command` (lines 422-437) only check:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5822149Z   ```python
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5822381Z   ("command", "bash", "powershell")
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5822652Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5822835Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5824592Z   This means VS Code-style hooks that use `windows`, `linux`, or `osx` keys will produce no action entries in the display_payload and appear as just `Hook file integrated: hooks.json` rather than showing an actual summary. Since the feature's stated security purpose is showing what will execute, missing OS-specific commands is a transparency gap — especially for the VS Code target which is explicitly mentioned in the comments.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5826282Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5826800Z   This is a recommended fix since the security claim is "shows what will execute", but it silently misses VS Code's OS-specific override keys.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5827397Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5827574Z   ### 2. Hook
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5827774Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5830395Z   The action lines break the existing tree grammar. The current tree uses `  |-- ` for child entries and `  |     -> ` for multi-target expansions, but the new hook action lines use `  |   ` which doesn't match either pattern — it looks like a continuation without the child marker semantics. The `(hooks.json)` suffix identifies the source hook filename, but the inconsistent prefix creates visual confusion in the tree structure. The same ambiguity applies to merged hooks files — the parenthetical could refer to either the source or the target file, since both `hooks.json` and `settings.json` serve as output paths. This is worth flagging as a clarity issue.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5832813Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5833668Z   There's also a problem where hook summaries get skipped entirely on re-installs if all the hook files were already adopted without needing fresh writes, leaving users without transparency about what happened during the installation.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5834594Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5834882Z   The `--dry-run` mode ha... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 6650 chars]

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5842951Z [304] ◐ Now I have a thorough picture of the code. Let me now formulate my findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5843359Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5843557Z   ## Summary of findings
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5843782Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5844029Z   ### 1. `_hook_verbose` split source of truth (recommended)
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5845417Z   At line 283, `_verbose = bool(getattr(ctx, "verbose", False)) if ctx is not None else False` is set as the function's single verbose source. All other primitives use this `_verbose` value consistently. However, the new hook block introduces `_hook_verbose = _verbose or (bool(getattr(logger, "verbose", False)) if logger is not None else False)` at lines 417-419.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5846725Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5846923Z   This introduces two issues:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5848454Z   - **Inconsistency**: The hook path expansion uses `_verbose` from ctx, but the action summary payload dump uses `_hook_verbose` which can pull from logger.verbose as a fallback. This means the hook header might be non-verbose while the payload dump is verbose if logger.verbose is True but ctx is None—behavior that diverges from the rest of the function.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5850305Z   - **Source of truth divergence**: Every other verbose behavior follows `_verbose` alone, but hooks add `logger.verbose` as an additional source. The proper fix would be to unify the verbose source.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5851900Z   - **Indent inconsistency**: The existing tree uses `  |-- ` for primitive headers and `  |     ` (5 spaces) for multi-target sub-items, but the new hook lines use `  |   ` (3 spaces) for event summaries and `  |     ` for raw JSON, creating misalignment.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5852811Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5853447Z   - **Raw JSON output**: There's no size limit on the verbose JSON dump, so pathological cases with large hook files containing embedded content could produce excessive output.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5854154Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5855213Z   - **Redundant verbose parameter**: The `verbose` check before calling `logger.verbose_detail()` is partially redundant since that method already gates internally on the verbose flag. The outer check only exists because of the `logger is not None` guard, so the function signature could be simplified.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5856335Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5856803Z   - **ASCII compliance**: All strings pass the ASCII-only requirement—no emoji or unicode characters.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5857270Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5857679Z   - **Action lines always emitted**: The action lines showi... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 10079 chars]
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5858088Z

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5865760Z [314] ◐ Now I have all the context I need. Let me compose the final JSON response.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5866156Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5866352Z   Key findings to report:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5867051Z   1. **recommended**: `display_payloads: list` in `IntegrationResult` — unparameterized, should be `list[dict[str, Any]]` or better a `TypedDict`.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5868369Z   2. **recommended**: `_iter_hook_entries` + `_summarize_command` only check 3 of 6 `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS` — `windows`/`linux`/`osx` hooks produce no action summary, silently falling to "Hook file integrated" fallback.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5869520Z   3. **recommended**: Payload rendering is gated on `_info["files"] > 0`, which excludes adopt-only re-installs where hooks are equally active.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5870505Z   4. **nit**: `_build_display_payload` is an instance method that only dispatches to static methods — could be marked as static itself.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5871495Z   5. **nit**: `_agg_hook_payloads` is allocated on every primitive iteration, but could be conditional on `_prim_name == "hooks"`.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5872044Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5872310Z   Now I'm preparing the mermaid diagrams as JSON string values.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5872651Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5874771Z   Mapping out the class hierarchy: `IntegrationResult` and `BaseIntegrator` are the base abstractions in base_integrator.py, with `HookIntegrator` extending the latter and `HookIntegrationResult` providing compatibility in hook_integrator.py. The `IntegratorBundle` frozen dataclass and `_log_hook_display_payloads` function live in services.py, and everything ties together through `integrate_package_primitives`. The key pattern here is that `IntegrationResult` uses a collect-then-render approach for display payloads, while `BaseIntegrator` establishes the contract for subclasses.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5876938Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5878495Z   Now tracing the actual execution flow: `integrate_package_primitives` routes to `HookIntegrator.integrate_package_hooks` or `_integrate_merged_hooks`, which rewrites the hooks data, writes it to disk, then builds the display payload through `_build_display_payload`. This builder-like method assembles the payload dict, and helper functions like `_iter_hook_entries` and `_summarize_command` handle the pure transformations along the way.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5880111Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5880483Z   The aggregated display payloads flow back up th... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3616 chars]

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5885403Z [320] ◐ Now I have a full picture. Let me construct my JSON response.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5885908Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5886117Z   I found these key findings:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5886364Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5887935Z   1. **RECOMMENDED**: `_iter_hook_entries` and `_summarize_command` only check `("command", "bash", "powershell")`, missing `"windows"`, `"linux"`, and `"osx"` from `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS`. These are legit VS Code OS-specific overrides that will be silently absent from the transparency output. A hook file that uses only `linux: ...` or `windows: ...` keys will display as "Hook file integrated: hooks.json" with zero action details, directly contradicting the security claim.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5890483Z   2. **RECOMMENDED**: The dry-run path skips hook action output entirely—it exits before any integration runs, so users never see what actions will execute before their first commit, and no warning is issued about this limitation.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5892394Z   3. **RECOMMENDED**: The `(hooks.json)` annotation is misleading since it shows the source filename but resembles a target path, yet the actual deployed file varies by platform (like `<pkg>-hooks.json` for VS Code or `settings.json` for Claude), creating confusion about what's actually being modified.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5894177Z   4. **RECOMMENDED**: The CLI documentation hasn't been updated to reflect the new hook action output lines that now appear in the install command, which violates the DevX contract requiring CLI changes to be documented in the same PR.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5896107Z   5. **NIT**: The command summarizer produces false positives when `.env` appears in shell pipelines—for example, `bash -c "source .env && ./run.sh"` gets tokenized as `runs .env` after quote stripping, hiding the actual script being executed, so I need to filter out single-segment tokens.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5897813Z   6. **NIT**: The hook action sub-lines use `  |   ` spacing, but the existing tree format uses `  |-- ` for children and `  |     -> ` for path expansion, so I should align the continuation prefix to match one of these patterns for consistency.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5898618Z

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5915256Z [323] ◐ Now I have a clearer picture. Let me analyze the key security issues:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5915641Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5916075Z   **Issue 1: `rewritten` dict is mutated after `_build_display_payload` call (for merged targets like Claude)**
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5916571Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5916780Z   Looking at `_integrate_merged_hooks`:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5917303Z   - Line 1292-1300: `rewritten, scripts = self._rewrite_hooks_data(...)` - creates deep copy with paths rewritten
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5918009Z   - Line 1303: `hooks = rewritten.get("hooks", {})` - gets reference to the hooks dict inside `rewritten`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5918670Z   - Line 1314: `for raw_event_name, entries in hooks.items():` - iterates entries from `rewritten`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5919429Z   - Line 1326-1328: The code mutates each entry dict by adding an `_apm_source` marker directly to the objects that `rewritten` references
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5920213Z   - Line 1424-1431: `_build_display_payload` is then called with this already-mutated `rewritten` dict
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5920677Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5921472Z   So the display payload gets built after the mutation happens, which means `rendered_json` includes those `_apm_source` markers. For schema-strict targets like Claude,
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5922162Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5922996Z   Stripping `_apm_source` before writing to disk means the file on disk won't have them, but `rewritten` still does since it was mutated in place. So `rendered_json = json.dumps(rewritten)` would show markers that aren't actually persisted.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5923921Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5925036Z   For non-schema-strict targets, the markers do get written to disk as part of the merged config, but the display payload is built from `rewritten` (just this package's hooks), not from the full merged file content. So the rendered output shows only this package's contribution, not what the merged file actually contains.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5926224Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5926914Z   The test acknowledges this distinction—it only verifies that rewritten paths are present, not that `rendered_json` matches the on-disk content for Claude.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5927572Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5929158Z   For VSCode, the test is stricter: it asserts that the payload equals what's on disk, which makes sense since each package gets its own separate JSON file. [truncated: showing first 2000 of 15710 chars]

2026-06-09T09:07:53.5975000Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5975515Z [343] ◐ Now I have a clear picture. Let me analyze the indent situation one final time:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5976272Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5976644Z   **Existing patterns:**
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5977207Z   - `  |-- ...` = 2 spaces + pipe + 2 dashes + space = primitive headers
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5978316Z   - `  |     -> {p}` = 2 spaces + pipe + 5 spaces + `->` + space = multi-target expansion sub-items (these appear under `|-- verb:`)
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5979335Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5979696Z   **New patterns:**
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5980260Z   - `  |   {event}: ...` = 2 spaces + pipe + 3 spaces = action summary lines
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5981219Z   - `  |   Hook file integrated: ...` = same
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5981777Z   - `  |   Hook JSON (...)` = same
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5982625Z   - `  |     {_jline}` = 2 spaces + pipe + 5 spaces = JSON content lines
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5983282Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5984896Z   So when I'm showing both the multi-target expansions and the hook details, the `->` lines sit at 5 spaces (deeper indentation), while the action summaries stay at 3 spaces, and the JSON content also goes to 5 spaces to align with the expansion targets.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5986903Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5989302Z   In the standard single-target case, this looks clean and follows normal tree structure. But in the verbose multi-target scenario, having the action lines appear at a shallower level than the path expansions creates a visual hierarchy problem—it's a real UX issue, though it only shows up in that edge case. I'll mark it as recommended since it's about getting the visual structure right in the verbose output.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5991050Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5992746Z   Now I need to check whether the action lines are being skipped when there are only adopted-only runs. I notice there are no tests specifically covering `_log_hook_display_payloads`, so the logging output itself isn't validated. I'm also realizing I need to check the severity schema — the task expects findings formatted with `blocking`, `recommended`, and `nit` levels rather than a different structure.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5994260Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5995755Z   Looking at my first finding about dual verbose sources, this is a `recommended` severity issue: `_verbose` from the context is the primary source, but `_hook_verbose` introduces `logger.verbose` as a fallback, which could cause divergence if they're ever out of sync. The `verbose_detail()` method already gates on `logger.verbose` internally, so having a separate `verbose` parame... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3188 chars]
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5997236Z 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5997353Z [344] ◆ ```json
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5997556Z   {
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5997768Z     "persona": "cli-logging-expert",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5998046Z     "active": true,
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5998776Z     "summary": "Two recommended fixes: unify the verbose source of truth for hooks (latent divergence risk) and align hook action indent with existing tree depth. Two nit-level polish items.",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.5999549Z     "findings": [

2026-06-09T09:07:53.6074027Z   1. **TERMINAL INJECTION (recommended)**: `_summarize_command` returns raw Python string (with actual ESC bytes) from `json.load`. `_log_hook_display_payloads` prints these directly to terminal without sanitization. `_emit_hook_event_diagnostics` guards the same data with `_sanitize_event_name` (0x20-0x7E filter) but `_build_display_payload`/`_log_hook_display_payloads` don't apply this guard. `rendered_json` verbose path is safe (json.dumps re-escapes ESC → \u001b).
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6076996Z   2. **`_apm_source` in rendered_json for Claude (schema-strict)** (recommended): The `rewritten` dict gets mutated in-place to add `_apm_source`, then `_build_display_payload` is called on it. For Claude, `_apm_source` is stripped before writing to disk, so the rendered JSON includes it but the on-disk content doesn't—and the test doesn't verify they match.

2026-06-09T09:07:53.6178092Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6178288Z   Key issues to weigh:
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6178642Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6179227Z   1. **Four-panelist convergence on HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS gap** (python-architect, devx-ux-expert, supply-chain-security-expert, cli-logging-expert): 
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6180137Z      - `_iter_hook_entries` and `_summarize_command` only check 3 of 6 HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS ('command', 'bash', 'powershell')
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6181065Z      - OS-specific hooks ('windows', 'linux', 'osx') produce zero transparency output
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6181824Z      - This directly contradicts the PR's stated security contract, though all panelists classify it as `recommended` rather than `blocking`
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6182424Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6184180Z   2. **Supply-chain-security findings** - two direct security contract breaks where the display format diverges from what's actually written to disk. The `_apm_source` key appears in rendered output but gets stripped before persistence, and the pre-transform format shown to users differs from the post-transform format stored on disk. These are marked `recommended` severity but they fundamentally violate the PR's core invariant that display_payloads faithfully reflects what's persisted.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6186018Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6186982Z   3. **Test coverage gap** - the headline feature `_log_hook_display_payloads` has zero test coverage, and the existing security contract tests show unknown outcomes because they haven't been run. This creates a regression trap on a surface that should be secure-by-default.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6188309Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6188742Z   4. **Documentation needs updates** across install, hooks-and-commands, and security-and-supply-chain docs.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6189248Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6189842Z   5. **ANSI injection vulnerability** via unsanitized command and event strings, plus the supply-chain-security expert flagging this as a critical issue.
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6190530Z   
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6191922Z   The core problem is that the PR claims a security contract: "display_payloads faithfully reflects what is written to disk and executed." But two code paths break this contract. For Claude targets, `_apm_source` appears in the rendered display but gets stripped from disk. For Gemini targets, the pa... 2026-06-09T09:07:53.6193522Z   {
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6194362Z     "headline": "Hook transparency delivers APM's strongest enterprise trust signal, but Claude/Gemini rendered_json divergence and a 3-of-6 HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS blindspot break the PR's stated security invariant before it can ship.",
2026-06-09T09:07:53.6203032Z     "arbitration": "This PR delivers what APM has been missing as an enterprise differentiator: a user-visible, per-event audit trail of exactly what each hook does at the moment it deploys. The oss-growth-hacker is correct that this is APM's strongest enterprise trust signal since the content scanner, and the core architecture (building `display_payloads` from post-rewrite data, the integration-tier test structure) is well-conceived. harshitlarl's community contribution rebased with authorship preserved is exemplary OSS hygiene that should be celebrated explicitly in the release note.\n\nThree concrete bugs make the stated security invariant false before merging. First, for Claude targets, `_build_display_payload` is called after `entry[_apm_source] = source_marker` mutates `rewritten` in-place, but before that key is stripped for the disk write in lines 1489–1494 -- so `rendered_json` carries `_apm_source` keys absent from the deployed `.claude/settings.json`. Second, for Gemini targets, `display_payloads` is built from pre-`_to_gemini_hook_entries`-transform data while the disk holds the renamed (`bash`→`command`), rewrapped, and millisecond-converted Gemini format -- a different schema than what the user sees. Both are direct violations of the PR's own invariant: 'display_payloads faithfully reflects what is written to disk and executed.' Third, four panelists independently identify that `_iter_hook_entries` checks only 3 of the 6 `HOOK_COMMAND_KEYS` -- hooks using `windows`, `linux`, or `osx` keys deploy correctly but produce zero transparency output, the precise failure mode the PR was built to prevent. The test-coverage-expert's `outcome: missing` on `_log_hook_display_payloads` (the headline user-visible function) compounds ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 9236 chars]

Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews.
